speaker: interviewer
text: February 23rd 1995, survivor is Sam Honigman. Interviewer is Susan Neshick. City is Miami, country USA. Language used is English. Tape number one. I want to ask a [INAUDIBLE]. Sorry, I want to ask-- can you start by telling me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Say my name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yiddish, it's Yishmuel. And the Ukrainian people used to call me Schmilic.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in English, they call me Sam, or Samuel Honigman.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your age, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 1927, that's-- I'm right now 67 years old. October the 1, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And the city and country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Miedzyrzecz. That's near-- near a bigger city, it's called Rowne. Until 1939, used to be Poland. And then after that, in 1939, if the Germans start fighting with the Polish, half of the country took away the Germans. And half of the country took away the Russians. We fell in in the Russian side.

speaker: interviewer
text: But at the time of your birth it was Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was Poland. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me a little bit about your parents and your family life when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was a child, mine-- we were very poor. Because what happened, my-- mind father slipped in ice, and his hip came out. And they didn't have no money to have operation. And the doctor said it cost so much. And they would have to sell the cow to make the operation. So mine grandmother ask the doctor what will happen if we wouldn't do it. So he said will be a cripple the rest of his life.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had to slave, very hard work, working for farmers. We had a garden. We had the cow, used to milk the cow. And we used to sell the milk in the city. Our city, our house was outside Miedzyrzecz. It was a half a mile. And my mother used to milk the cow and sell the milk. When I used to ask here before I used to go to cheder-- mother, can I have a glass of milk? She used to take a little milk and make it white with water.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just she says, mine dear son, if I'll give you all the milk, I will have nothing what to make butter of it. And I have nothing to sell, and we need yeast to-- to bake bread. And that was our life story. We used to live into little rooms with a kitchen and one of the-- one living room, bedroom combined together. I used to sleep with my mother-- with my father. And my two sisters used to sleep with my mother. The bed was like straw and boards. And every Friday, she used to go ahead and put in, like, a mud, a yellow mud on top. It didn't have any wooden floor or any carpeting like that. And it was very cold. And she used to always used to have to go ahead and melt the ice before we wash the hands. Because we used to have to go to the-- to the pumper or-- to get some water. And she used to feed the cow. We had a horse. My father used to work by the farmers and earn some potatoes or wheat. My mother, my grandmother, my [NON-ENGLISH], my grandfather used to sleep in a bed in the kitchen. And that's the way we brought up. But we were happy. My father was a very religion man. He used to go every day to shul. And-- or, he didn't go to shul, so used to put me in the horse and in the cow and to go to the pasture. Started up when I was six years old. Till 1942, [NON-ENGLISH], they were talking to the Germans came in. And we were ready to run together with the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, a lot of Jewish people mixed in, as I said, [NON-ENGLISH]. What are they going to do to you, the Germans? You still going to work for the farmer. They wouldn't touch you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And before you're turning around, was February 14, 1942. The Germans came in, lots of dogs and everything. The first day we came in, we were like in a highway house. They snapped into the house. My father was standing in davening with the tallis and tefillin. I swear by God, that breaks my heart when I talk about it. [CRYING] I saw it myself. He came up. And my father, he took out the knife. He cut up his beard for my father. He cut the tefillin. And unfortunately, they raped my sister. She was 16 years old. They beat out the hell of you. My mother was screaming. She was fainting. I run out of the house. And my middle sister run with me. And-- [CRYING] [SOBBING] sorry to say it-- [CRYING] sorry. [SOBS] [SIGHS] We came back, my sister laying, screaming. The second day came in our good neighbors. That's why I believe in that thing to say again. Don't believe your neighbors. They threw us out in the house. They took away the cow, the horse, the chickens. We had ducks. And we walked to my auntie, to my father's sister, to the city. And we lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, it was the-- your neighbors--

speaker: interviewee
text: The neighbors, they took away--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that took away--

speaker: interviewee
text: --everything. They were-- took away the house and everything there. We lived there. And we used to go-- should I continue? We used to go to work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of work? They made-- they made even the Jewish people cleaning the toilets. Not just cleaning the toilet, but-- pardon me, we didn't have no toilets. The dirt, everything, slaving, beating up, everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the German people are the way elected police, the Ukrainian police. And the-- the Ukrainian police made Jewish police without ammunition. But instead of going around and looking for labor, they used to come up to the committee and say, today we need 200 people to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- the same week, they came out. They said all the men should come out in the middle-- in the middle of the city. And they picked up the strongest 200 people, including one of my uncle, [INAUDIBLE]. And what city were you in and this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Miedzyrzecz.

speaker: interviewee
text: In--

speaker: interviewee
text: All in Miedzyrzecz. And they went away. And they said they going to take them to Kiev to work, to slave there in the field, in the forest. That was a lie. They took them out only not even 5 miles in the same grave. They digged a small grave where tried another the 3,000 people, these 200. Because they didn't want these strong people in case they're going to fight.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- they did it mostly in these small towns that way. So they took out the cream, the strong men, right away. Because they could fight, I mean, you know. So they-- after that, Ukrainian people told us quietly they were killed there, all kind, slice up with knives, whatever. And they made them digging the grave, and they buried them. That was the first week. And when was-- do you remember what year this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942. I would think. I don't know. Probably, it was February the 14th. So it probably would be February the 8th? The 26th? Or the 25th? I couldn't say exactly. That was in 1942. No, of course, we lost my aunt, my uncle, and one of my cousin, the strong guy. The woman up till day, she was crying. And I'll-- I'll say later on about the woman what happened by the grave. Or, I could say it right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] His wife, that he was killed, when she saw me running for the grave, running-- she knew that I'm not trying. Maybe God should help me. I was always a runner. Up till today, I say only thing I'm scared is for God. Otherwise, it doesn't matter what kind. you know, I just go. I say, die, die. She took out her engagement ring, and so pushed me into that thing. That engagement ring help me getting-- once I was very hungry, I mean dying and everything. That helped me getting two loaves of bread. She give it to me. And up until today, I have a-- a ring, a piece on chest. I didn't want to take it with me. But I carry her name, because she-- that was my auntie.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can we go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then coming back to that thing, what happened they-- they went away. And they-- that's the way it was going on. It was going on up to the first day of [NON-ENGLISH]. We were living there. It was already less 200 people. So we were over-- approximately 2,800 in Miedzyrzecz. Till one day, we were children, they used to take in walking to pick fruit. We had a lot of apple and-- and cherries and plums. And that we went to pick them for the farmers. And the Germans used to give us to police for that thing, some kind of a bread and things like that for the work. One day we saw coming up five, six trucks. And they didn't go fast, because we had mud. We didn't have anything like a highway. And I came up to the-- to the main one that he was the leader, [INAUDIBLE]. That was [INAUDIBLE]. His son is the one in Israel that he lost his high. He was the leader, the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked him, [INAUDIBLE], what's the trucks doing there? He says, oh, they just passing by. When they came close, and they picked us up, all the children. And they were going towards that place where the 200 Jewish people are buried.

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard screaming, screaming, Jewish. And I see close-- maybe a quarter of a mile-- people digging graves. I was lucky. God was with me. I was in the end of the truck. I jumped out from the truck and start running. I'm a runner. My poor sister, Rifka, she was in the back of the truck. She saw, she-- that's the one, she was raped. She saw me running. She wanted to jump out. I heard a shot. They shot her. They start shooting after me. There was another man running. He was older with two years, and probably around 14. And it was a lot of bushes. We went in between the bushes. And the bullet didn't strike me. Like I said, it's a God. I was hiding between there about three days. Then we were hungry. I went to farmers. It was helping me. I speak with their language. Some farmers was scared. They wanted to help, I wouldn't just ignore them all. But they were scared, because the Germans were saying like that if they catch anybody helping a Jew, they going to be instantly killed. And they did it. So what happened, we learned how to steal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you with at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: With that man.

speaker: interviewee
text: With that man.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, his name is Dudela, Duder. Duder, I don't remember his second name, but that was his, Duder. He was, after that, he was killed in the second time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then what happened? We asked one of our elderly lady, what's new in Miedzyrzecz? She said in Miedzyrzecz, there is already Jewish people sleeping the streets. And they-- they are alive, some of them. So I figured we going to walk back to the city. Coming about a half a mile from the city, was going-- was going in a bicycle two police, Ukrainian police with rifles. They catched us. They beat us. They beat me up. And they beat up my friend in the face. And they tied us up for the hand, one to the other one. And we were running in the back of their bikes. He brought us up-- I'll never forget that thing-- a heavy-- a heavy leader, a German with Nazi, [GERMAN] over their face. And they give us two brooms and sweep the sleep. Plus, we have to clean some of the bodies. I came up to two people that I knew them. They were laying dead. And I was anxious to find out if mine parents-- and he was anxious, that other one, Dudela, if his parents are there. As soon nobody was there, of course the officer didn't come out to see, with two [INAUDIBLE], we threw away the broom. We run. We run to the house. In my auntie's house, we were preparing ourselves. If we don't want to go to work, we used hide ourself in our cellar. We dig the cellar underneath the bed. And we were hiding there. For it was for-- we digged it for weeks, you know, with a shovel, with everything there. And we were there. So I figured like that. If I go right away into that cellar, my mother had a weak heart, she would faint. And so I was slowly come around and say, momma, it's Schmilic, mama, it's Schmilic. I heared quietly, my father said to the mother, it's Schmilic. And my mouth says, [NON-ENGLISH] The goyim [NON-ENGLISH] in Yiddish. The Ukrainian people want to find out special. Because a lot of Ukrainians, they spoke Jewish. So I give you all kind of sign, and she had start crying. [SOBBING] My mother was so excited that she took me by the hair, and I was bleeding my head, she pulled me down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then what happened, she start bugging me, asking me, where is Rifka? I fooled her for almost [SOBBING] three days, that Rifka's alive, and she's hiding herself. But that [INAUDIBLE] Ukrainian purpose.

speaker: interviewee
text: That time they went away, they made a ghetto. They put up wires around two streets, because it was left that time they slaughtered 2,200. No, was 2,000. 2,000 and-- or a little bit over 2,000. And then it was left, 800 people. And one night, we see my mother is not in bed with [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the 800 were now moved into the ghetto? The 800 remaining people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we all slaughtered there. Yeah, it was all that thing. And everybody leave for the [INAUDIBLE]. And my mother went, runned away to the-- they were watching, but not so closely to the wires. And she walked to that Ukrainian man, and I saw it. I started running over there and watching. My mother was sitting by the Ukrainian man. He did not touch her. He gave her something to eat. And she find out that the [INAUDIBLE] is there, that my sister is dead. My father knew about it, and my middle sister Dvoira knew about it. My mother, I didn't want to tell her. She had a weak heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I walked with her back at nighttime. The Ukrainian fellow was nice enough. He gave us some food. You know, he would have be killed for that. And we walk back. And that was going on [INAUDIBLE] to Succos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Succos, unfortunately, the first day of Succos came around four [INAUDIBLE] with big-- that's always four German people, SS. And they gave an order to take all whatever is left, walking to the graves barefoot. Some of them, they took the-- by trucks, most of them by foot. Whoever couldn't walk, they were slaughtering with knives, with bullets, and threw them in the ditch all the way there. From Miedzyrzecz through Niewirko, it will say right in there, probably in [INAUDIBLE]. That's the place where they have buried the 3,000 men. Niewirko is the place that the Jewish men had a factory that they used to make stones, bricks, [BACKGROUND NOISE] full with sand-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] [INAUDIBLE] --and full with sand. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And what happened there, that's-- they were easy to dig. And they were right in there starting to dig the graves. And I was one of them. And it took, I would say, around eight hours. And we dig the graves. And between the digging to graves was a lot of killing, a lot of scream. Because a lot of people didn't want to dig the graves. Because they knew that they digging for themselves the graves. You have to make the grave like that, going down with steps. And the top is a hill. That's what-- my children saw it. We have a map of everything there. We drawed a map. Like I mentioned before, my son is a beautiful city planner. He was an architect, making all the design to make that stone. So you saw the beautiful view of mountains. They were standing with four machine guns, shooting down to the graves. I would say most of them was alive. And they were screaming. They took like a-- a special chemical-like, it looked like a paint. They spilled over, the bodies shouldn't smell. After, the ground was still lifting up, was very little ground. That's why they were using it as garbage to dump. Because half of them, the bones, of course, are still there. Because I saw with my own eyes. I was kissing the bones. My son kept on crying. [SOBBING] I was crying, I said, I kissing my parents bones. So what happened there, I saw what happened. So I start running again. They shot me. And in front of the-- it's like from here to the lobby, that's all-- there's a ditch. Surprisingly, there's still some water. When I went with my two beautiful children, [SOBBING] when I saw that ditch, I laid down. They couldn't get me off. [SOBBING] It reminded, the way I was standing, I was shot with [INAUDIBLE] was somebody getting killed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After that, it took-- it took about two hours. It quiet down. I lift up my head all the way-- [SIGHS] [SOBBING] was nobody there. I was alone. [SIGHS] I came out to the grave. I looked in it. I was debating if I should lay down and die there. [SOBBING] Something happened to me. And I figured to myself, no, life go to go on-- [SOBS] I'm sorry-- I start walking. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I walk about a half a mile. I saw two boys walking there. One is holding eye like that with some of his-- part of his pants. He was shot. He lost his eye. He's in Israel now. That was Monek Averbuch, older with two years than me. The other follow was alive. He runned away probably while he was walking to the grave, there on the way. These two boys were city boys. I never knew them before. They couldn't talk too good in Ukrainian. It helped me, because I grew up between Ukrainian in the village. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we start [INAUDIBLE]. So up till today, one of them, Monek Averbuch, said to me, Schmilic, my-- let's go to the police station. And they are going to kill us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to him, you know what, Shmonek? [NON-ENGLISH]. [SOBBING] Life has got to go on. Maybe God saved us. We have to see what we could do. So up till today, when I'm in Israel, many times, when I come over there, he-- the man is over 6 foot.

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife always says to him-- to me, you know, Schmilic, I live with my-- my husband for years. I never saw Monek crying. Each time when you come to Israel, he kicks me out of the bed. And he wants to sleep with you, and he always cries. He remembers the days. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: What we went through together. Unfortunately, that didn't take two weeks. There's a whole saying, three's a crowd, two's companies. When we used to go-- day-- day time we used to hide itself. It shows right there on the picture that I showed it to you. We used to hide in the straw, digging out in the straw [INAUDIBLE] day time.

speaker: interviewee
text: At nighttime, we used to go to steal. How we used to steal? Summertime was beautiful. Wintertime, we used to go and try to get in, like I told you on the telephone, to the bounds where the Ukrainian people used to mix in food for the pigs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the biggest enemies were dogs. They used to bark. We used to try to kill the dogs. Mine-- mine-- Monek is one of the biggest killer. Unfortunately, he's very good nature. But maybe he killed a lot of Arabs and things like that. Because to him to kill a person is like nothing. I would-- I'm not a killer. So, he's strong, God bless him. He used to go ahead and try to choke the-- the dog. They used to bite him. But he used to put in [INAUDIBLE]. Anyway, he used to get rid of the dogs. And then when I used to go in at night time. We used to pick [INAUDIBLE], because it was around Miedzyrzecz. And I knew Ukrainian. [INAUDIBLE]. We went into elderly lady. Some of the older lady used to throw you outside a piece of bread or something just to get rid of yous. And some of them, they used to sneak into the house. While I was talking to them, he used to push in whatever used to see, clothing and anything to thief. We became thieves. And then later on, that's the way we survive. One day, the man came over. We came over in that [INAUDIBLE]. We looked in the guy, the guy is dead. So we lost our third man. He-- he got sick, and he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long had you been in hiding--

speaker: interviewee
text: In hiding I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was hiding like that almost two and a half years, till a half a year before the liberation. I-- we got involved, unfortunately-- fortunately, I should say that way. We were walking in the forest. We were in a lot of forest. Start shooting off-- us two people. And we thought it's the Ukrainian police.

speaker: interviewee
text: And fortunately, it was partisans. Because that, they didn't shoot-- the Nazis, shoot high. And they told us how to go to catch the-- the partisans. They saw that we are Jewish. One looked like Jewish, but I couldn't say that. They didn't say, look, you have a Jewish face. But he talk fluently Ukrainian. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He speaks probably Russian, too. But he saw that's far easier for me to talk Ukrainian. It's one language is similar to the other. He spoke to me Ukrainian. And they took us in with both hands. And I was forced off my feet. They helped me. And after that, we were six months with the partisans. That was a-- a blessing. We had it very good. It was dangerous. Everyone made it, and that's the way we liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still in the forest in hiding when you were with the partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We were there. And we came over to small-- small little city, Miedzyrzecz. The first day when we came in, mine-- I told him-- maybe I shouldn't say that. But I told the officer who was the police, some of them. They took 28 police, and they pulled up with two trucks. And the aimed them, and I spit at them. I didn't tell them exactly that they were killers from the Jews. That's the truth. They will killers from the partisans, they took their heart. And they did have no trial. They instantly, they didn't have no time. Because of the war was still going on. The war was still going on in Rowne. Rowne is only 40 miles from Miedzyrzecz. And they were so hard knocking that when we slept in the police station, we were scared to come sleep anywhere, too. The windows were slotted out. It was bombs, everything there. But I figured we're not going away no place. Because if they have to run back, we'll run with the partisans. They were good ammunition and everything there. And then after, they arrested a lot of Ukrainian people. I don't know what they did. But there they sent them to Siberia. Because they were taking it-- and they did not play around. Mine friend, he was so bitter. When he used to go around in the basement when the police didn't check him, he used to have a small little knife, very, very sharp. And he used to cut the meat.

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to say like that. I said, Monek-- later on they didn't let him-- why are you cutting so badly? He says, I want to see them bleeding like they bleed my parents. I feel good when I see their blood. And that's exactly what happened. And up till today, he was scared. When we were over there, he was scared in Miedzyrzecz. He went with me, by the way, in that thing. He was scared some Ukrainian will kill him. You know? But unfortunately, he's still scared. Because if somebody would recognize him-- he's a redheaded one-- he would be in trouble. They wouldn't touch me [INAUDIBLE], because I wasn't in-- mind you, I'm not hiding. But I haven't got the heart to do this kind of stuff, you know. After that-- we-- should I continue that way? Let me go back over a couple of things if you could. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK. Let's--
